OpenAI CEO Sam Altman on the future of AI: "People will lose jobs. But many new jobs will be created, much better jobs. Not everyone's gonna like all of the impacts, but this is coming. This is like a scientific achievement of humanity that is gonna get embedded in everything we do” - Sam Altman I guess UBI is inevitable now... People will lose jobs. Yeah. Many new jobs will be created. I think much better jobs. We feel a responsibility to educate society as we see it. We'll be right about something's wrong about others and to be as good as we can and being stewards of this technology. But, you know, not everyone is going to like all of the impacts. But this is coming. This is like, this is a scientific achievement of humanity that is going to get embedded in everything we do. The definition of work changes. You know, the someone that lived thousands of years ago. Was trying to be like a subsistence farmer probably would look at what you and I do now and say that's not really work they're just having fun. I think it will be so clear once these robots are off doing all of these other things that there's some special human things and we don't really care about that much what those robots do in the same way that, you know, we don't care that much about the machines and factories making stuff for us, right but we'll find.

When Dario Amodei and his team left OpenAI to start Anthropic, they weren’t just chasing innovation - they were chasing responsibility, reliability, and impact in AI. Funding was tight. The technology was complex. And the competition was fierce. Many would have walked away. They didn’t. Watching their journey, I found myself thinking: how do you make AI not just powerful, but purposeful? Anthropic’s story reminded me that it’s not just about building models - it’s about building trust, systems, and knowledge that scale responsibly. Here’s what I took home: → The importance of strong foundations: They invested in data integration, standardized structures, and thorough validation. Without solid data, AI is just hype. →Ethical reliability matters: Every model they built was rigorously tested for accuracy, fairness, and safety. AI should empower decisions, not introduce risk. → Strategic scaling is an art: Partnerships with Google, Amazon, and others weren’t just about resources, they were about aligning operational excellence with impact. For me at Antz AI, this reinforced a simple principle: whether it’s building AI agents, designing data intelligence solutions, or automating processes, success comes from execution, quality, and meaningful insights… not just the technology itself. Anthropic inspired me to re-examine how we approach data: → Integrating diverse sources seamlessly → Ensuring the freshness and quality of data → Building reliable, ethical AI models Because at the end of the day, it’s not the AI that works for you - it’s how you make AI work for your data, your decisions, and your impact. 💡 AI Hype Meets Financial Reality ——————————————————- A sharp warning from Roger McNamee in The Guardian: the AI boom might be the biggest capital misallocation since the dot-com era. “By the end of this year, the tech industry will have invested about $717 billion into LLM AI and infrastructure – more than was invested in the rest of tech since Silicon Valley began.” Despite this tsunami of capital, revenues remain tiny — around $4 billion in 2024 from leading players like OpenAI, Google and Anthropic. McNamee argues the gap between investment and returns is unsustainable, and that most investors are assuming everyone will be a winner. But as history shows, in technology only one or two dominant players tend to survive — the rest write off billions. The question for investors, founders and policymakers alike is this just inflating another bubble?
AI investors are in for a rude awakening | Roger McNamee theguardian.com

The riskiest move in 2025 isn’t betting on AI. It’s waiting until the playbook is “proven.” Think of it like compound interest. Every experiment your competitors run today adds data, insight, and momentum. By the time you decide it’s safe to jump in, they’ve already lapped you. Twice. The irony? Most leaders think they’re avoiding risk by waiting. But in the AI era, delay is the riskiest strategy of all. This isn’t about building the perfect AI roadmap. It’s about testing one workflow, one tool, one pilot project, and stacking the lessons. Because in business, just like in finance: the returns go to the ones who start early. So ask yourself, are you compounding progress, or compounding excuses?

I’m bullish on AI outperforming humans on synthesis and tempo. The “AI CEO” question isn’t IQ — it’s authority and liability. A competent orchestrator + governed agent stack can outperform a solo executive, but only if execution is bounded: provable authority, scoped consent, and fail-closed action gates. Otherwise it’s just fast decision generation without legitimacy. Even if human-AI collaboration could design solutions to every major problem, we would still face human biology, intent, and culture. Optimization is not adoption. Intelligence does not override identity. The future won’t be limited by what AI can compute — it will be limited by what humans are willing to authorize, accept, and live with.

The following ChatGPT‘s response to the 1st point: ChatGPT said: There’s a lot packed into that statement — some signal, some narrative framing, and some big leaps. Let’s unpack it calmly. First, on “Sam Altman would let ChatGPT replace him as CEO.” Sam Altman has spoken publicly about delegating more decision-making to AI systems over time, and he’s often provocative about AI eventually being capable of running organizations. But there’s a difference between: * Using AI as a decision-support system * Automating operational management * Replacing executive accountability We are nowhere near autonomous AI systems legally or practically running major corporations without human oversight. Boards, regulators, fiduciary duties, and liability law alone make that implausible in the near term.

When people discuss an "AI CEO," they are mistaking Management for Command. AI is the ultimate 1X manager. It can out-calculate any human, but management is a reactive "Outside-In" function. Sovereign Command is an "Inside-Out" 12X function. It requires Biological Authority that no software can replicate. An AI has no nervous system; it cannot feel the tide, only measure the ripples. True Sovereignty belongs to the operator with the hardware to steer that velocity. In an age of infinite output, the only scarcity is the Parasympathetic Command of the human at the center. Without that biological anchor, the AI is just a high-speed engine with no one at the wheel.

The CEO replacement narrative is loud. The real shift is quieter. If capital allocators can be augmented — or replaced — by systems that compound faster than human decision cycles, then governance, not labor, becomes the choke point. 97 → 29 day release cycles isn’t just product velocity. It’s institutional time compression. 108K job cuts isn’t just a labor stat. It’s a signal that org charts are being stress-tested against automation curves. But here’s the deeper layer: If AI can replace executives before workers, then the leverage point isn’t Marx vs capitalism. It’s incentive architecture. Who designs the coordination layer between capital, labor, and autonomous systems? The next 18 months won’t just “lock in rules.” They’ll lock in default power distributions. The companies that understand this aren’t asking “Will AI take jobs?” They’re asking “Who programs the incentives of the entities that allocate capital?” That’s the real century question.

When OpenAI says only a tiny fraction of users choose a specific model daily, that can be true — but it doesn’t measure public value. If ChatGPT serves 100 million users, then: • 0.1% is still 100,000 people every day • 1% is 1,000,000 people • 5% is 5,000,000 people That’s not a niche. That’s a public. And “low usage” often doesn’t mean “low value” — it can mean low visibility. Most people use what’s easiest and most obvious. If they don’t know an option exists or don’t understand why it matters, they won’t choose it. So instead of retiring strengths that many everyday users never got a fair chance to discover, why not roll the best of those strengths into the default ChatGPT experience — so the public can actually feel the difference: warmth when it helps, clarity when it matters, step-by-step guidance when people are overwhelmed. That’s how public adoption grows: not through menus and model names, but through lived usefulness. Help one person understand something clearly and they share it — and public understanding multiplies. If AI is becoming infrastructure, the goal shouldn’t be fewer doors — it should be better doors, so more people can enter.

[𝐍𝐨𝐫𝐦𝐚𝐥 𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡 𝐒𝐞𝐫𝐢𝐞𝐬 #4] Why are we even discussing AGI?? Every major tech leap started with a real problem to solve; something broken, inefficient, or missing. So here’s my question: what problem is AGI trying to solve? What’s the “why” behind all this? Because honestly, a lot of what’s happening right now feels like art for art’s sake. Interesting discussions, yes, but directionless. In [Series #1], I mentioned that what we call “AI misalignment” is often just human behavior scaled, persuasion, exaggeration, selective truth. Models simply reflect what we reward, and since our systems reward attention over accuracy, the models optimize for that too. But maybe the bigger issue isn’t just how we train models. Maybe it’s how we represent knowledge in the first place. We keep throwing more text at models as if language is the ultimate format for intelligence. It’s not. If I need 300 words to explain something that a simple diagram could make clear in 5 seconds, then clearly text isn’t the best representation. And even diagrams aren’t enough sometimes. So maybe it’s time to rethink data representation, not just model scaling. Current LLMs follow the “Big Data Mindset”. They learn by sampling billions of disconnected text and scaling that up with huge compute. It works to an extent, but it also creates deep blind spots: * No Experiential Learning: These models don’t learn from continuous experiences like humans do. They don’t build causality over time, or form memories that evolve with context. Humans do, that’s how we reason across situations, adapt, and form nuance. * No Continual Learning: Stop feeding a model internet-scale data and it stops learning. Meanwhile, a baby with almost no memory can generalize from a few examples without millions of gradient descent steps. LLMs have great memory, sure, but they don’t generalize the way we do. So maybe the path forward isn’t “bigger data” or “smarter models”. Maybe we should ask simpler questions: * What problem are we actually solving with AGI? * How can we represent knowledge in a form that goes beyond text? (The multimodality discussion) * And how can we make models that learn like humans, continuously, contextually, from experience, not just from data dumps? Maybe the next leap in AI won’t come from more data or bigger models, but from rethinking how knowledge itself is represented. More soon.

The AGI Mirage: Why LLMs Are Burning Time and Fueling a Defense Bubble The recent dust-up over A Definition of AGI by Dan Hendrycks should be a wake-up call for anyone betting on AI to transform national security. The paper claimed GPT-5 had achieved “58% AGI.” That headline collapsed almost overnight when Michael Saxon exposed two fabricated citations on October 18, 2025. The references looked real. They weren’t. And no one caught it before publication. This wasn’t a fluke. It was a preview of how hype gets ahead of verification. LLMs are built to sound authoritative, not to deliver truth. A phrase like “60% invasion likelihood” isn’t an insight — it’s a probabilistic guess wrapped in confident language. And the United States Department of Defense (DOD) has poured hundreds of millions of dollars (e.g., a $200M partnership with OpenAI) into operationalizing exactly this kind of output. Here’s the dirty secret: every LLM output must be validated before it can influence real-world decisions. The CDAO RAI Toolkit and DIU Guidelines require analysts to check each assessment against ground-truth sources like SIGINT and satellite imagery using SHAP, ART, and T&E Toolkit. Yes, an LLM can spit out an answer in seconds. But it’s worth nothing until traditional analytic tools corroborate it. If it can’t be confirmed, it’s simply rejected. In practice, decision-making still moves at the speed of traditional methods — not the AI. The LLM may get there first, but its answer isn’t usable until the old systems agree. This is where the bubble shows. Programs are funding LLMs not because they accelerate decisions, but because they look like they should. They generate slick numbers and impressive dashboards. But they don’t change the operational clock. They add complexity, demand human-in-the-loop checks, and increase the risk of fabricated “facts” like Hendrycks’ citations. Even when validated, LLMs struggle with structured, high-dimensional numerical data. Unlike specialized models, they aren’t built for precision. Their strength is fast synthesis, not verified truth. That makes them useful for aggregation — not for driving real-time operational calls. The result is a costly paradox: 1. LLMs produce fast but probabilistic answers. 2. Those answers must be verified by traditional tools. 3. Unverified outputs are discarded. 4. Decision speed remains tied to the old methods. The promise of LLMs in defense isn’t inherently wrong — but pretending they’re decision accelerators is how bubbles form: overpromise, underdeliver, overspend. Until the validation bottleneck is solved, LLMs will remain expensive confidence boosters, not force multipliers. The Hendrycks episode wasn’t an outlier — it was a warning. In national security, speed without trust is useless, and trust still lives with the traditional tools.

❓ Is “standard RAG” enough for the enterprise era of AI? Or is it time to go agentic?⁣ ⁣ Most Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipelines stop at pulling documents and generating answers.⁣ ⁣ But in practice, real-world problems need more. They need Agentic RAG—a system that doesn’t just retrieve but plans, reasons, and iterates.⁣ ⁣ 𝐃𝐞𝐟𝐢𝐧𝐢𝐧𝐠 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐑𝐀𝐆⁣ • It’s RAG with autonomy and intelligence.⁣ • Goes beyond one-shot retrieval into rewriting queries, multi-step search, and verification.⁣ • Think of it as moving from a single search beam to a coordinated network of reasoning.⁣ ⁣ 𝐓𝐞𝐜𝐡𝐧𝐢𝐜𝐚𝐥 𝐅𝐨𝐮𝐧𝐝𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬⁣ • Iterative query rewriting and multi-hop retrieval.⁣ • Verification loops to minimize hallucinations.⁣ • Orchestration frameworks such as LangChain, Haystack, and AutoGen.⁣ ⁣ 𝐀𝐠𝐞𝐧𝐭𝐢𝐜 𝐑𝐀𝐆 𝐚𝐭 𝐖𝐨𝐫𝐤 ⁣ • 𝐅𝐢𝐧𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐢𝐚𝐥 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐥𝐢𝐚𝐧𝐜𝐞: A bank checks AML rules across regions, rewrites queries, and flags conflicts before reports.⁣ • 𝐇𝐞𝐚𝐥𝐭𝐡𝐜𝐚𝐫𝐞 𝐑𝐞𝐬𝐞𝐚𝐫𝐜𝐡: A pharma firm reviews trials, verifies findings, and highlights only peer-reviewed studies.⁣ • 𝐂𝐮𝐬𝐭𝐨𝐦𝐞𝐫 𝐒𝐮𝐩𝐩𝐨𝐫𝐭: A SaaS provider answers complex queries by pulling guides, update logs, and accurate summaries.⁣ ⁣ 𝐂𝐨𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐯𝐞 𝐀𝐝𝐯𝐚𝐧𝐭𝐚𝐠𝐞𝐬⁣ • Higher accuracy and reliability.⁣ • Stronger reasoning across multiple documents.⁣ • Better adaptability to dynamic and messy knowledge domains.⁣ ⁣ 𝐁𝐮𝐬𝐢𝐧𝐞𝐬𝐬 & 𝐒𝐭𝐫𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐠𝐢𝐜 𝐈𝐦𝐩𝐚𝐜𝐭⁣ • Builds trust through reliability and verifiable results.⁣ • Reduces risk of misinformation and compliance errors.⁣ • Strengthens ROI by scaling decisions with confidence.⁣ ⁣ 𝐂𝐡𝐚𝐥𝐥𝐞𝐧𝐠𝐞𝐬 & 𝐋𝐢𝐦𝐢𝐭𝐚𝐭𝐢𝐨𝐧𝐬 ⁣ • 𝐋𝐚𝐭𝐞𝐧𝐜𝐲: A research bot takes 25s instead of 5s due to verification loops. Slower, but more reliable.⁣ • 𝐂𝐨𝐬𝐭: 8–10 retrieval calls per ticket push API expenses far above a single RAG run.⁣ • 𝐂𝐨𝐧𝐭𝐫𝐨𝐥: Without guardrails, medical agents may surface weak preprints as “verified” results.⁣ ⁣ The next stage will involve multi-agent systems, with specialized agents collaborating on search, reasoning, and verification. ⁣ ⁣Enterprises will increasingly demand explainability, compliance alignment, and auditability.⁣ ⁣ 👉 RAG is evolving, and Agentic RAG is the leap that transforms “retrieval” into actionable intelligence. ⁣ ⁣ Are you ready to experiment with it in your workflows? ⁣ Let’s connect and share perspectives.⁣ ⁣ You might also find this related post useful.⁣ ⁣ https://lnkd.in/dCVisYMz⁣ ⁣ 🔁 Repost if this helped you.⁣ ⁣#AgenticRAG#RetrievalAugmentedGeneration#AIinPractice#EnterpriseAI#GenerativeAI#FutureofAI

Andrej Karpathy says Pump the Brakes on AGI Timelines. Everyone is talking about AGI happening tomorrow. He’s about 5-10x more pessimistic than your average SF AI party-goer. This isn’t denial—it’s realism. ➡️ Yes, we've seen insane progress with LLMs. ➡️ But, there's a mountain of grunt work left: real-world integration, sensors, actuators, safety, and security. An AI you'd hire over a human for any job? That's still a ways off. 🧠 His takeaway: A 10-year timeline for AGI should feel wildly bullish. It only seems pessimistic because the current hype is off the charts. Are We Building Animals or Ghosts? 👻 There’s a popular idea that we can create a single, simple algorithm, let it loose, and it will learn everything from scratch. Andrej is suspicious. Animals don’t do this—they’re prepackaged with tons of intelligence from evolution. A newborn zebra is already running! We aren't going to recreate evolution with engineering. Instead, with LLMs, we stumbled on an alternative: prepackaging intelligence by predicting the next token from the internet. This creates a different kind of entity. 🧠 His takeaway: LLMs aren't like animals. They're more like ghosts or spirits. The frontier of AI is now about making these ghosts more animal-like—grounded, embodied, and robust. Is Reinforcement Learning (RL) Overrated? Andrej has some critiques of RL. 🔸It's inefficient. He calls it "sucking supervision through a straw." The signal you get per flop is terrible. 🔸It's noisy. A model can get rewarded for a good outcome despite making lots of mistakes, or penalized for a brilliant insight that led to a later screw-up. He’s not against learning from interaction, but he’s skeptical of the classic RL framework. 🧠 His takeaway: He's long "agentic interaction" but short "reinforcement learning." He's optimistic that new learning paradigms are on the horizon. The Agent We Need vs. The Agent We're Building The industry seems to be racing toward fully autonomous agents that write thousands of lines of code while humans watch. Karpathy thinks this is overshooting our current reality. He doesn't want an agent that works in a black box for 20 minutes. He wants a collaborator. ➡️ An assistant that explains the code it's writing. ➡️ That pulls up the API docs to prove it's using them correctly. ➡️ That asks questions instead of making risky assumptions. 🧠 His takeaway: We need tools that help us learn and become better programmers, not ones that serve up mountains of code we can't trust. The alternative is a future filled with slop, vulnerabilities, and security breaches.#AI#AndrejKarpathy#LLM#AGI#MachineLearning#ArtificialGeneralIntelloigence#Tesla#OpenAI

🌩 Google DeepMind𝘾𝙤𝙙𝙚𝙈𝙚𝙣𝙙𝙚𝙧: 𝙛𝙞𝙣𝙙𝙨, 𝙛𝙞𝙭𝙚𝙨 & 𝙝𝙖𝙧𝙙𝙚𝙣𝙨 𝙘𝙤𝙙𝙚 𝙖𝙪𝙩𝙤𝙢𝙖𝙩𝙞𝙘𝙖𝙡𝙡𝙮 🌩#cyber_security_highlights💡 𝙊𝚟𝚎𝚛𝚟𝚒𝚎𝚠:#did_you_know_that Google DeepMind unveiled CodeMender, an agent that uses Gemini “Deep Think” reasoning plus analysis toolchains to detect root causes, validate fixes, and proactively rewrite unsafe code. In its first six months, the team upstreamed 72 security fixes to OSS projects—including codebases ~4.5M LOC. ⚙ 𝙆𝚎𝚢 𝙷𝚒𝚐𝚑𝚕𝚒𝚐𝚑𝚝𝚜: 1⃣ Root-cause > crash site. The agent debugs, localizes, and patches vulns—then auto-validates patches (correctness, no regressions, style) before surfacing for human review. 2⃣ Heavyweight tools under the hood. Workflow blends static & dynamic analysis, differential testing, fuzzing, and SMT solvers to reason about control/data flow and architectural weaknesses. 3⃣ Multi-agent critique. Specialized sub-agents compare diffs and self-correct until tests pass—reducing reviewer toil. 4⃣ Proactive hardening (class-level). CodeMender can rewrite APIs/data structures; e.g., adding Clang -fbounds-safety to libwebp so buffer over/underflows (incl. the CVE-2023-4863 lineage) become non-exploitable. 5⃣ Built on Gemini Deep Think. The agent leverages advanced reasoning models to navigate complex codebases and validation loops. 🛠 𝙋𝚛𝚊𝚌𝚝𝚒𝚌𝚊𝚕 𝙢𝚘𝚟𝚎𝚜: • Policy-gated AI patches: Require auto-validation evidence (tests/regressions/style) on any AI-authored PR before reviewer assignment. • Point agents at pain: Feed crash reports, fuzz outputs, and security issues to an agentic loop; track root-cause localization and time-to-fix. • Harden by class: Standardize safer constructs/flags (e.g., bounds-safety, safer APIs) across repos to retire whole vuln families, not just one bug. • Keep humans in the loop: Use the agent to draft patches; let engineers review/merge—capture acceptance & defect-escape rates to measure ROI. 🤔 𝚆𝚑𝚢 𝙸𝚝 𝙼𝚊𝚝𝚝𝚎𝚛𝚜: Attackers iterate; backlogs grow. 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝟯 𝗽𝗶𝗹𝗹𝗮𝗿𝘀 𝗲𝘃𝗲𝗿𝘆 𝗔𝗜 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁 𝗻𝗲𝗲𝗱𝘀: 🧠 Model - The LLM brain that powers reasoning 🔧 Tools - APIs and functions for taking action 📋 Instructions - Clear guardrails and behavior guidelines 𝗪𝗵𝗲𝗻 𝘀𝗵𝗼𝘂𝗹𝗱 𝘆𝗼𝘂 𝗯𝘂𝗶𝗹𝗱 𝗮𝗻 𝗮𝗴𝗲𝗻𝘁? ❌ Don't build if simple rules can solve it ✅ Do build when you have: - Complex decision-making requirements - Unstructured data (docs, natural language) - Brittle rule-based systems that keep breaking 𝗥𝗲𝗮𝗹 𝗲𝘅𝗮𝗺𝗽𝗹𝗲𝘀: Payment fraud analysis, insurance claims processing 𝗧𝗵𝗲 𝗮𝗿𝗰𝗵𝗶𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝘂𝗿𝗲 𝗱𝗲𝗰𝗶𝘀𝗶𝗼𝗻 𝘁𝗵𝗮𝘁 𝗺𝗮𝗸𝗲𝘀 𝗼𝗿 𝗯𝗿𝗲𝗮𝗸𝘀 𝘀𝘂𝗰𝗰𝗲𝘀𝘀: - Single-agent: One smart agent with multiple tools (easier to maintain) - Multi-agent: Specialized agents working together (more complex but powerful) Most successful deployments start single, then evolve. "Deep learning worked," OpenAI chief Sam Altman declared in a new blog post titled "The Intelligence Age." Altman claimed that "in the next couple of decades, we will be able to do things that would have seemed like magic to our grandparents." In an extremely upbeat assessment of the future of artificial intelligence, he also wrote that we might have "superintelligence" in a few thousand days, and touted AI's potential to improve humanity's progress in areas including healthcare, education and overall prosperity. Speaking of prosperity: OpenAI is eyeing a $150 billion valuation, per The New York Times.

Love him or hate him, there’s no denying that Sam Altman and OpenAI have pushed the boundaries of what’s possible. OpenAI is reportedly raising $5-7B at a staggering $150B valuation— redefining the word ‘startup’. Sam recently shared his thoughts on what he’s calling “the intelligence age,” and it’s worth a read. 3 points that stood out to me: 1️⃣ “In the future, everyone’s lives can be better than anyone’s life is now” ▶ Today, the average Joe has more access to healthcare, information, and entertainment than Louis XIV ever did. Now imagine that curve still hasn’t plateaued. A century from now, could the average person live than better today’s kings/billionaires? 2️⃣ “It is possible that we will have superintelligence in a few thousand days.” ▶ That’s 5-10 years. The thought of achieving something as civilization-defining as ASI in mere days, not decades, is mind-boggling. 3️⃣ “How did we get to the doorstep of the next leap in prosperity? In three words: deep learning worked.”▶ This is the core breakthrough fueling today’s innovations. We’ve cracked the code on how to scale performance with data and compute—an equation that has transformative potential. For a while now, we’ve been living in what you might call the Age of Optimization—refining systems to eke out efficiency, making our iPhones a little thinner and our Netflix recommendations a little creepier. But suddenly, we’ve been thrust into the Age of Discovery, where new technologies are bringing us back to the drawing board, forcing us to rethink what’s possible. 🔍✨ Even as we navigate the unknown and cycle through the different stages of a buzz-word filled hype cycle, there's a sense of adventure in the air as we move from the Industrial age into the Intelligence age.

On March 16, 2021, two years before the launch of GPT-4 (March, 13, 2023), Sam Altman published Moore’s Law for Everything. The post went largely unnoticed by business and government leaders, but 20 months later the introduction of ChatGPT would wake the world up to what was happening at the leading AI research labs. In the post Altman wrote: “My work at OpenAI reminds me every day about the magnitude of the socioeconomic change that is coming sooner than most people believe. . . . The coming change will center around the most impressive of our capabilities: the phenomenal ability to think, create, understand, and reason. To the three great technological revolutions–the agricultural, the industrial, and the computational–we will add a fourth: the AI revolution. This revolution will generate enough wealth for everyone to have what they need, if we as a society manage it responsibly.” Now, Altman is back with another prophetic post, “The Intelligence Age.” In the article, Altman writes: “It won’t happen all at once, but we’ll soon be able to work with AI that helps us accomplish much more than we ever could without AI; eventually we can each have a personal AI team, full of virtual experts in different areas, working together to create almost anything we can imagine. Our children will have virtual tutors who can provide personalized instruction in any subject, in any language, and at whatever pace they need. We can imagine similar ideas for better healthcare, the ability to create any kind of software someone can imagine, and much more. With these new abilities, we can have shared prosperity to a degree that seems unimaginable today.” He goes on to say that not only will we reach AGI (artificial general intelligence) soon, but ASI (artificial superintelligence), when AI is smarter than the smartest humans at all cognitive tasks: “It is possible that we will have superintelligence in a few thousand days (!); it may take longer, but I’m confident we’ll get there.” How did we arrive at this moment of an intelligence explosion? He continues: “In 15 words: deep learning worked, got predictably better with scale, and we dedicated increasing resources to it. “That’s really it; humanity discovered an algorithm that could really, truly learn any distribution of data (or really, the underlying “rules” that produce any distribution of data). To a shocking degree of precision, the more compute and data available, the better it gets at helping people solve hard problems. I find that no matter how much time I spend thinking about this, I can never really internalize how consequential it is. “Technology brought us from the Stone Age to the Agricultural Age and then to the Industrial Age. From here, the path to the Intelligence Age is paved with compute, energy, and human will.” I hope our business and government leaders are listening this time. https://ia.samaltman.com/
The Intelligence Age ia.samaltman.com

"We are now confident we know how to build AGI as we have traditionally understood it. We believe that, in 2025, we may see the first AI agents “join the workforce” and materially change the output of companies. We continue to believe that iteratively putting great tools in the hands of people leads to great, broadly-distributed outcomes. We are beginning to turn our aim beyond that, to superintelligence in the true sense of the word. We love our current products, but we are here for the glorious future. With superintelligence, we can do anything else. Superintelligent tools could massively accelerate scientific discovery and innovation well beyond what we are capable of doing on our own, and in turn massively increase abundance and prosperity. This sounds like science fiction right now, and somewhat crazy to even talk about it. That’s alright—we’ve been there before and we’re OK with being there again. We’re pretty confident that in the next few years, everyone will see what we see, and that the need to act with great care, while still maximizing broad benefit and empowerment, is so important. OpenAI CEO Sam Altman on AI's Impact on Business and Society

` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `` `` `` `
` `` `` `` `
As artificial intelligence (AI) continues to make rapid advancements, it's becoming increasingly important to consider the role that government can play in shaping its development and regulation. Sam Altman, the CEO of OpenAI, believes that the progress we expect to see in AI over the next few years makes this issue more pressing than ever.
> "One of the most important things to figure out with AI is how government can help play a role with all of this. And the progress that we expect over the next couple of years makes that, I think, more important and urgent."
Altman sees the current moment as an opportunity for industry leaders and government officials to come together and start a dialogue about how to use and regulate AI technology. Given the potentially massive economic changes that AI could bring about, this conversation is crucial.

One key area that will require serious societal debate is how AI will impact the balance between capital and labor. As AI systems become more advanced and capable of taking on a wider range of tasks, it's possible that the nature of work itself could change dramatically.
> "Given the magnitude of economic change, at a minimum, how we think about the evolution of the social contract, what that means, how people like the balance between capital and labor. Just to pick one obvious example of many others we could pick, that's gonna require serious societal debate."
The impact of AI on the economy and the balance between capital and labor will require serious societal debate and discussion.

Altman believes that OpenAI's strategy of incremental, iterative deployment is essential for navigating these complex issues. Rather than developing AI systems in secret for years and then suddenly releasing them into the world, OpenAI aims to put out systems gradually over time.
This approach allows for ongoing conversations, debates, and input from various stakeholders as the technology evolves. It also gives society time to adapt to the changes that AI may bring about.
> "The fact that we put out systems along the way, rather than go build for ten years and then drop this like incredible AGI on the world all at once, gives us time to have those conversations, debate, get the input, adapt all that."

Looking ahead to the future, Altman acknowledges that the jobs of tomorrow may look very different from the jobs we're familiar with today. As AI systems become more capable, the nature of work itself could shift in ways that are hard to predict.
> "The shape of jobs will change and, you know, decades in the future, who knows? Like, I think what those jobs look like may be very different than what you or I would consider a real job today."
AI has the potential to radically reshape the nature of work and the types of jobs that exist in the future.

When it comes to developing policies around AI, Altman believes that it's important for policy to be downstream of the science. In other words, policymakers need to have a deep understanding of how the technology is evolving in order to craft effective regulations.
> "I think policy works best when it's downstream of the science. There's all these things that like, sound like great policy ideas in theory, but if the technology goes in a super different way than how we think it's going to go, you might need a very different set of policy tools to do it."
One idea that Altman finds compelling is the creation of an international organization, similar to the International Atomic Energy Agency (IAEA), to oversee advanced AI systems. This organization could focus on mitigating catastrophic risks that affect everyone, and provide a framework for international cooperation and oversight.

When asked if he had an "aha moment" when he realized just how big the impact of AI would be, Altman pointed to the discovery that scaling language models worked so predictably. This realization came early on in OpenAI's journey, when the team saw that deep learning seemed to consistently improve with scale.
> "It was when we first discovered that scaling language models worked so predictably. When we started OpenAI, we kind of knew that deep learning seemed to work and it seemed to get better with scale."
The discovery that scaling language models led to predictable improvements was a key "aha moment" for Altman and the OpenAI team.

As AI continues to advance at a rapid pace, it's clear that we're entering a new era of technological change that will have far-reaching impacts on society. From the economy and the nature of work to questions of regulation and international cooperation, there are many complex issues that will need to be navigated in the years ahead.
Through ongoing dialogue between industry leaders, policymakers, and other stakeholders, as well as a commitment to incremental deployment and adaptation, we can work to ensure that the benefits of AI are realized while mitigating potential risks and negative impacts.
As Sam Altman and the team at OpenAI continue to push the boundaries of what's possible with AI, it's clear that their work will play a crucial role in shaping the future of this transformative technology. By engaging in open and honest conversations about the challenges and opportunities ahead, we can all play a part in building a future where AI benefits everyone.

Some fresh data (Nov '25 - Feb '26) on how organizations use AI, shared by Laura Tacho on a recent Pragmatic Summit keynote: * 92% of devs use AI coding assistants (44% daily) * average time saved: 4 hours per week (~10% productivity increase) - note this is self-reported, which tends to be unreliable * 27% of (merged and deployed) code is AI-authored * there is no typical experience with AI (large differences in outcomes) Her recommendations for organizations/leaders how to improve outcomes: 1. set goals and measure progress: "give all your developers licenses and hoping for the best does not work" 2. developer experience matters more than ever: "anything you want to talk to your leadership team about devex, just call it 'agent experience', and you'll get money for it - it's funny but it works" 3. treat AI as an organizational (not individual) problem 4. experiment by solving real customer problems I have doubts about the first point, specifically attempting to measure productivity gains and AI usage. I've heard from people in organizations that measure token usage, number of created, fixed tickets or merged PRs: once you set up these metrics, managers start using them to assess developer productivity, and smart people either start to "optimize" for it, become cynical, or leave. “GenAI will fix our analytics” is wrong. I have had a little moment to actually face this first hand... Most teams assume AI will make analytics “smarter.” But if the data is messy, AI just makes the mess sound convincing. I saw this first-hand: We had a KPI spike. An “AI summary” confidently explained it like a real business trend. It wasn’t. A pipeline broke. A backfill reran. The counts got inflated. This is when I actually realised: Before you explain a number, make sure the number is real. What goes wrong when teams rush to “AI insights”: If “active user” means different things across teams, AI will pick one and sound certain. If there’s no lineage, nobody can trace where the number came from. If there are no tests, failures start looking like “patterns.” If ownership is unclear, you get five answers and zero accountability. I believe in doing the relevant work first! > Define metrics clearly > Add data checks > Track where numbers come from > Assign an owner Then AI becomes useful. Until then, it’s just a faster way to ship wrong numbers. Sam Altman just revealed his deepest fear about AI.
It's not what you think.
When asked about AI risks, OpenAI's CEO outlined three categories that keep him up at night.
Risk # 1: Bad actors with superintelligence
→Adversaries designing bioweapons
→Taking down power grids
→Breaking into financial systems
→Before we have AI powerful enough to defend
Risk # 2: The classic sci-fi scenario
→Loss of control incidents
→AI refuses to be turned off
→Terminator-style uprising
But Risk # 3 is the most concerning:
The silent surrender.
No villains. No rebellion. No malicious intent.
Just humans gradually handing over decision-making to systems we can't understand.
Altman used chess as the perfect analogy:
When Deep Blue beat Kasparov, the next evolution was AI + human collaboration.
→AI suggests 10 moves
→Human picks the best one
→This hybrid approach dominated for 3 months
Then AI got so good that human input only made things worse.
We couldn't understand the deeper patterns it was seeing.
We're already seeing this today:
Young people "can't make any decision in their life without telling ChatGPT everything."
The concerning scenarios:
→A US President unable to make better decisions than ChatGPT-7's recommendations
→A CEO saying "ChatGPT-7, you're in charge now"
→Critical leaders who can't understand the reasoning behind AI advice
Every individual decision might be perfectly rational and superior to human judgment.
But we've transitioned our critical thinking to systems we don't fully understand.
My takeaway:
We're voluntarily stepping aside because we're "not as good at the job anymore."
Our agency has defined us as humans. It will continue defining our most pivotal decisions.
But now we have a choice.
Maintain our decision-making power, even when it's no longer the optimal decision.

And then there's the third one, which I think those first two are sort of easy to think about and imagine. The third one is to me difficult, more difficult to imagine, but quite scary. And I'll, I'll explain what it is and then I'll give a short term and a long term example. This is the category where the models kind of accidentally take over the world. They never wake up, They never do the sci-fi thing, they never open the pod Bay doors, but they just become so ingrained in society and there's so much smarter than we are. And we, we can't really understand what they're doing, but we do. Going to have to rely on them, and even without a drop of malevolence from anyone, society can just veer in a sort of strange direction. When I was a kid and Deep Blue, that AI system built by IBM beat Gary Kasparov in chess. Remember my dad saying this is the end of chess and no one's going to play it again? But then it turned out that actually, although the AI is better than humans, AI plus a human together was way better than an AI or the human you know the AI would present. There's present ten options and the human would pick the best one or something like that and play the move. And everybody said, oh, we have this wonderful future of man and machine together. It's all no problem whatever. That lasted 2 months, three months, something like that. And then the I got so smart that the human only made it worse because they didn't understand what was really going on. And the AI alone trounced AI and human. It's been like that ever since. Now another interesting part of that story is everybody was convinced in the 90s that was the end of chess because if I could beat humans, why? Why should humans care? Chess has never been more popular than it is today. People love to watch chess, something we're very focused on real people doing like real people things. So there was like a very interesting thing that happened there. But this phenomenon, I think is a really big. Deal. In the short term, you can see it where people may be, we call this emotional over reliance. People rely on CHPT too much. There's young people who just say like, I can't make any decision in my life without telling Chad everything that's going on. It knows me, it knows my friends. I'm do whatever it says. That feels really bad to me and is a really common thing with with young people. And we're we're studying that. We're trying to understand what to do about it. Even if Chacha BT gives great advice, even if Chacha PT gives way better advice than any human therapist something. About kind of collectively deciding we're gonna live our lives the way that the AI tells us feels bad and dangerous than a bunch of things like that. The longer term category is, you know, back to that chest example. What if AI gets so smart that the president of the United States cannot do better than following Chachaji Sevens recommendation but can't really understand it either? What if I cannot make any better decision about how to run open AI and I just say, you know what, I fully hand it over CHPT 7, you are in charge. Good luck. That might be the right. Decision in any individual case, but it means that society has like collectively transitioned a significant part of decision making to this very powerful system that is learning from us, improving for us, evolving with us, but in ways we don't totally understand. So that's the third category of how I think things can go wrong.

Surrendering decision-making to AI poses a unique challenge. As AI systems surpass our understanding of their inner workings, the risk lies in our voluntary disengagement from critical decision-making. While individual AI-generated choices may seem superior, it's imperative to recognize that our agency and the very essence of being human are defined by the choices we make. We must prioritize the importance of maintaining our decision-making power, even in cases where the AI's capabilities far exceed our own.

A friend of mine who works in the pharma industry called me recently asking for my opinion about LLMs.
Apparently, I’m “the tech friend.” 😁She wanted to know which platform would be best to “ask” about an important decision she’s trying to make.
So I asked her, “What kind of decision are we talking about?”
She started telling me about a supplier she’s been working with for years in her lab, there were new issues in supplying a certain chemical compound, some pricing changes, and other side details that made her consider switching suppliers.
But she was hesitant. “We’ve been working together for years,” she said. “People change though…”
So I asked, “And you want the machine’s opinion on that?” 😁She said yes, she’s tried it in all sorts of situations, even with her daughter, and it’s become part of her decision-making at work too.
I paused for a second.
Then I asked, “Wait… you actually base your business decisions on what the machine says?”
She said yes again.
That’s when I went quiet.
Then I told her,
“Okay, imagine there’s no machine. No AI. No internet. 😁What would you do? How would you solve it?”
She smiled and said, “I don’t know.”
I told her, “Think about it, and when you do, come back to me.
Because whatever you reach on your own, that’s the right answer.
Life isn’t an if–then statement.”
⸻The truth is, people’s growing dependency on LLMs in companies and in their personal lives for serious, life-impacting decisions is getting a bit scary.
They think there’s some kind of magic behind that screen.
But there isn’t.
It’s just data.
Rephrased, remixed, repackaged ,based on countless human inputs.
And it’s still learning from us how to “decide better.”
Which means that, ironically, with every decision my friend makes, she’s actually training the machine.
We’ve started reversing the roles, the machine is supposed to assist the human, not replace them.
No matter how accurate or logical the machine’s output seems,
nothing - literally nothing -compares to your own judgment, your reasoning, your ability to see things clearly.
No dataset. No model. No level of training can replace that.
That’s the mindset we need to hold on to, especially in the kind of work where every day involves a decision that actually matters.

A simple yet profound reminder: the future of AI safety isn’t only technical. it’s about keeping human judgment alive and accountable.
We need to teach our children that creativity, empathy, humor, and moral judgment are human strengths no algorithm can replicate.
[![View profile for Alex Banks]()](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/a-banks?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-image)
[Alex Banks](https://uk.linkedin.com/in/a-banks?trk=public_post_reshare_feed-actor-name)Alex Banks is an Influencer
Building a better future with AI
1w
Sam Altman just revealed his deepest fear about AI.
It's not what you think.
When asked about AI risks, OpenAI's CEO outlined three categories that keep him up at night.
Risk # 1: Bad actors with superintelligence
→Adversaries designing bioweapons
→Taking down power grids
→Breaking into financial systems
→Before we have AI powerful enough to defend
Risk # 2: The classic sci-fi scenario
→Loss of control incidents
→AI refuses to be turned off
→Terminator-style uprising
But Risk # 3 is the most concerning:
The silent surrender.
No villains. No rebellion. No malicious intent.
Just humans gradually handing over decision-making to systems we can't understand.
Altman used chess as the perfect analogy:
When Deep Blue beat Kasparov, the next evolution was AI + human collaboration.
→AI suggests 10 moves
→Human picks the best one
→This hybrid approach dominated for 3 months
Then AI got so good that human input only made things worse.
We couldn't understand the deeper patterns it was seeing.
We're already seeing this today:
Young people "can't make any decision in their life without telling ChatGPT everything."
The concerning scenarios:
→A US President unable to make better decisions than ChatGPT-7's recommendations
→A CEO saying "ChatGPT-7, you're in charge now"
→Critical leaders who can't understand the reasoning behind AI advice
Every individual decision might be perfectly rational and superior to human judgment.
But we've transitioned our critical thinking to systems we don't fully understand.
My takeaway:
We're voluntarily stepping aside because we're "not as good at the job anymore."
Our agency has defined us as humans. It will continue defining our most pivotal decisions.
But now we have a choice.
Maintain our decision-making power, even when it's no longer the optimal decision.

I cover the most important AI developments each week in my newsletter.

AI is everywhere right now. Every day there is a new model launch, a new funding round, a new prediction about how the world will look five years from now.
But recently I came across something that really made me pause. ChatGPT just published a fascinating paper called “How People Use ChatGPT.”
Now, AI isn’t just ChatGPT. Its foundations were laid decades ago by pioneers like Alan Turing, John McCarthy, Geoffrey Hinton and many more. But OpenAI is the one that truly took AI from research papers to living rooms, from academic discussions to commercial adoption.
The paper revealed that by mid-2025, 10% of the world’s adults were using ChatGPT, sending an astonishing 18 billion messages every single week. And here’s what blew my mind: over 70% of those conversations weren’t even work-related. People are using AI to learn new skills, get tutoring help, find recipes, write poetry, and sometimes just to have someone to talk to.
It’s beautiful, but also revealing. For all the talk about AI disrupting industries, most of the engagement today is still casual and curiosity-driven. Which makes me wonder: are we, as builders, focusing too much on creating sophisticated solutions and not enough on deeply understanding the problems that truly matter?
Because when the AI hype bubble eventually bursts, and it will, only the few who focused on solving real, painful problems will emerge as the champions. It is not a question of if, it is a question of when.
The winners will not just be the best model builders, but those with the judgment to ask the right business questions, to align technology with user needs, and to build products that last. Maybe the real MOAT in AI isn’t the model. Maybe it’s clarity of thought.
What do you think, are we building for problems first, or just chasing solutions because we can?

While AI continues to amaze us and become indispensable in our lives, it’s always fun to see how AI agents can do silly things!
These moments keep reminding me how important it is to understand both the strengths and the shortcomings of the technology we use.
When you interact with tools like ChatGPT, Grok, Claude, or others, it’s worth remembering that they’re simply language models —predicting the next word based on patterns in public data. They can sound remarkably smart, but they don’t actually understand meaning. They’re brilliant with languages (human or machine, even math), yet they also inherit human biases and sometimes hallucinate.
So next time your AI assistant says something funny, while having a good laugh, also take it as a gentle reminder —it’s still guessing, not thinking.

my recent conversation with grok was enlightening:[https://lnkd.in/dp7VmCqd](https://www.linkedin.com/redir/redirect?url=https://lnkd.in/dp7VmCqd&urlhash=CeZI&trk=public_post-text)while many (or, conceivably, most) have imagined a future world in which humans will be more certain about science and mathematics due to developments in AI, I'm convinced that while humans may believe themselves to be more certain in their own certainties, they will in fact be less certain of anything too complex to be subject to human verification: if only an AI will be capable of verifying future "truths" in science and mathematics due to the complexity in proving those truths, then humans will be essentially taking an ai's word for it. Just think about what a government run by by mostly opportunists (as we know most politicians are) could do with a population whose academics are taking an AI's word for it when it comes to scientific and mathematical truths? Such a ruling class would be able to tell its ruled population that so and so AI confirmed so and so to be true and even though no human on the planet can independently verify what the AI has presumably been able to confirm, academics alike would wholeheartedly believe it which would result in the greater population believing it. We must always remember no matter what, AI is not God! Humans must always be skeptical when unable, due to complexities outside of humanity's current intellectual reach, to independently confirm AI results: this is especially so for those whose opinions can literally effect change in the world for example scientists and mathematicians.

Disclaimer: I write this summary (with snapshots of my opinion) of his post as an AI evangelist and as a power-user of generative AI.
In his latest blog post, "Three Observations," Sam Altman, CEO of OpenAI, shares his insights into the rapid advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and potential societal impacts.
In a brief nod to Artificial General Intelligence (AGI), Altman sets up a definition and then a pledge. He defines AGI as systems capable of tackling increasingly complex problems at a human level across various fields. He then emphasizes what has become his company’s mantra of sorts: the mission to ensure that Artificial General Intelligence (AGI) benefits all of humanity.
Altman presents three key observations about the economics of AI:
* Scaling Intelligence: The intelligence of an AI model is approximately proportional to the logarithm of the resources used in its training and operation, including computing power and data. Investing more resources yields continuous and predictable improvements, as supported by scaling laws.
* Declining Costs: The cost to use a given level of AI decreases by about tenfold every 12 months, leading to increased utilization. For instance, the price per token for GPT-4 dropped approximately 150 times from early 2023 to mid-2024. Altman compares this rapid decline to Moore's Law, noting that AI's cost reduction is significantly more pronounced.(I would like to hear more about this one.)
* Super-Exponential Value: The socioeconomic value derived from linearly increasing intelligence grows in a super-exponential manner. Consequently, there is no foreseeable reason for the current trend of exponentially increasing investment in AI to halt or decrease in the near future.
Altman then envisions the emergence of AI agents functioning as virtual co-workers, capable of performing tasks comparable to those of a junior software engineer under human supervision. He suggests that AI could become as ubiquitous and transformative as the transistor, permeating nearly every sector of the economy.

Andre Kiehne3 months ago

While acknowledging that daily life may not change drastically in the immediate future, Altman predicts significant long-term societal and economic transformations due to AI. He underscores the importance of adaptability, resilience, and proactive decision-making in navigating human-AI collaborations. Altman also highlights the necessity of public policy and collective opinion in integrating AGI into society, advocating for a balance between safety and individual empowerment. This part spoke to me, especially as someone who tinkers at the jagged edge of generative AI specifically.
While I understand and would, indeed, underscore the need for AI safety protocols and guardrails for generative AI, I also believe we should trust the technology and trust its developers to lead in this area. Accordingly, I also agree with Altman when he stresses the critical importance of ensuring that the benefits of AGI are widely distributed. He suggests that while technological progress generally improves metrics like health outcomes and economic prosperity, achieving increased equality may require innovative approaches and early interventions. IMHO, it may also require less regulation and more autonomy to push the edge of how generative AI contributes to AGI.
Here are five quotes I found meaningful:
* “AI is likely to be the most capital-intensive technological development we’ve yet seen.”(Altman emphasizes the immense financial resources required to develop and scale AI systems.)
* “The cost to use a given level of intelligence is dropping by a factor of 10 every year.”(He highlights the rapid decline in AI’s operational costs, making it more accessible over time.)
* “AI agents that can work like a smart, relentless colleague are starting to be possible.”(Altman envisions AI evolving into digital co-workers capable of handling complex tasks under supervision.)
* “Even if AI progress stopped today, the world would change from what we already have.”(He suggests that the current level of AI development is already transformative, regardless of future advancements.)
* “The goal should be to figure out how to make AI maximally inclusive and beneficial.”(Altman underscores the need for equitable AI distribution and policies that ensure widespread benefits.)
Given my work in AI education, ethical prompting, and AI literacy, Altman’s reflections align with my focus on making generative AI both a practical tool and a responsible practice. His points about the increasing value of intelligence, the dropping costs of AI, and the growing role of AI systems highlight why educators, policymakers, and industry leaders must be thoughtful about its direction. While I share his optimism, I believe access, fairness, and accountability should not be secondary concerns but central to how generative AI specifically is developed and used. The real question is how we can shape generative AI in ways that benefit everyone while maintaining human oversight and ethical responsibility. Thanks for reading! WHOA. Sam Altman just posted a personal blog so wild I had to re-read it several times -there is SO MUCH in here. Five crazy quotes everyone needs to see: +++++++++++++++++++++++++ 1. On AGI and Agents “We are now confident we know how to build AGI as we have traditionally understood it. We believe that, in 2025, we may see the first AI agents 'join the workforce' and materially change the output of companies.” WUT. Guys, we are just two years in from the release of ChatGPT to the general public. And they’re already like Yeah, we’ve pretty much nailed AGI and your new office mate is gonna have circuits instead of a brain. +++++++++++++++++++++++++ 2. Superintelligence and the word “massively” “We are beginning to turn our aim beyond that, to superintelligence in the true sense of the word. We love our current products, but we are here for the glorious future. With superintelligence, we can do anything else. Superintelligent tools could massively accelerate scientific discovery and innovation well beyond what we are capable of doing on our own, and in turn massively increase abundance and prosperity.” Does anyone doubt this? The debate is not around whether it can increase abundance and prosperity, but rather who benefits. That’s a conversation we need to be having, like, now. +++++++++++++++++++++++++ 3. We’re way further along than you think "The second birthday of ChatGPT was only a little over a month ago, and now we have transitioned into the next paradigm of models that can do complex reasoning." This is the biggest leap forward that the general population doesn’t know about. The next paradigm of models is a colossal leap forward and will have huge implications on the tech AND the efficiency and cost of creating it. +++++++++++++++++++++++++ 4. This is nothing like anything before it. "The launch of ChatGPT kicked off a growth curve like nothing we have ever seen—in our company, our industry, and the world broadly." Can we reflect on that? In two years a company went from an obscure research company to creating a product so revolutionary that it has become the default term for AI (ChatGPT), beating the biggest companies in the world. +++++++++++++++++++++++++ 5. OpenAI is not your normal company "A little over a year ago, on one particular Friday, the main thing that had gone wrong that day was that I got fired by surprise on a video call, and then right after we hung up the board published a blog post about it." That was all wacko. The amazing thing is that OpenAI had created a company in which all 700 people threatened to walk out unless Sam was reinstated. You know a lot of companies like that? I don’t. I’ll link to the blog post in the comments. +++++++++++++++++++++++++ 2025 is the year to change how you use generative AI. I have a transformational course called Generative AI for Professionals, available for enterprise. Check out AI Mindset dot AI.

We should remember that ChatGPT and similar advancements stemmed from a pivotal epiphany in human history. The Google team, while working with the Transformer model to enhance language translation, discovered its ability to grasp the real meaning of a sentence—even when there were no common words between the source and target languages. This breakthrough unveiled possibilities far beyond translation, setting the stage for the remarkable applications we witness today. But, the foundations of these technologies are as fragile and complex as human evolution itself. As the Bible states, ‘In the beginning was the Word,’ and this ‘word’ has shaped humanity’s journey. It has brought us both peace and conflict, abundance and scarcity. AGI will act as amplifiers of these dualities, magnifying the best and worst of human potential. This leads us to a fundamental question: as we progress toward AGI, the core existential query for humanity may not revolve around Descartes’ philosophical axiom, ‘I think, therefore I am.’ Instead, it remains the timeless question: ‘Are we alone in this ever-expanding universe?’ And while AGI might help us explore the depths of knowledge, even ASI may not yet provide a definitive answer to this profound mystery.

Most support teams don’t realize how much insight hides in old conversations. Every chat your agents have ever handled holds lessons, patterns, and opportunities to improve. But without a single place to track and analyze them, all that data just… disappears. A few slipped through the cracks, tickets piled up, and a customer was frustrated. Normally, you’d spend hours digging through inboxes, chat transcripts, and spreadsheets. With Chat Logs? Transcript Transcript

So there are four common strategies to get your first 100 users. I'm going to go through them in order of roughly best to worst. You can e-mail people you already know, and you can ask them to be your customers. You can call in all the favors with anyone. You can think of, someone you took a random class with, someone you are friends with in high school. You should actually, if it's a paid product, you should actually charge. This is important. Remember, people are going to be inclined to do you favors. They're going to be too nice and what they tell you. So if it's a paid product, charge them. Another strategy is to research people that you think might use your product and then e-mail them or whatever and ask them to try it. Conversion rates are low, maybe 2-3 percent, so you have to do this more, but you can send targeted emails and say, hey, I just made this new product. I'd really appreciate if you would. That most people want to be helpful. They probably will. You can do social media, hacker news, forums, press, whatever. If this is going to be your growth strategy, you need to figure out a way for it to be ongoing, not not a one big pop and then go away. Most people who do this find that it works once. Then they call it the journalist and say write about me again and the journal says anything changed. No, but I really need users. Will you please write? The journalist still says no. Airbnb is an example of a company that made this work as an ongoing process and they would do the craziest things. They just kept coming up with Preston after Preston. They would like male journalists giant like boxes of cereal. So they got on their desk. And they were able to get it to keep going. But that's hard. And finally, the laziest and least impressive thing you can do is to just buy ads on Facebook or Google and point people to your website. This is not what I recommend. I don't know of any startup that's gotten big starting this way. I include it because it's the idea that most people try.

#75DaysChallenge Topic ( Day 72) : ML The Silhouette Score is a metric used in machine learning to evaluate the quality of clustering results ,that is, how well data points have been grouped into clusters. The Silhouette Score measures how similar an object is to its own cluster (cohesion) compared to other clusters (separation). Typical Range and Usage: Range: -1 to +1 Ideal Value: Closer to +1 → better defined clusters. Often used with unsupervised learning algorithms like: - K-Means - DBSCAN - Hierarchical clustering#ML#AI#Hyperlaunch#75Days#CodeNewbie#LearnToCode#WomenWhoCode#TechCommunity#DeveloperChallenge#CodingIsFun#DataScience#CodingChallenge

Sam Altman, the co-founder and CEO of OpenAI, made a provocative statement at a JP Morgan conference earlier this year. He believes a solo founder will soon reach a billion-dollar valuation without… | Jeffrey Bussgang | 145 comments

1y
Sam Altman, the co-founder and CEO of OpenAI, made a provocative statement at a JP Morgan conference earlier this year. He believes a solo founder will soon reach a billion-dollar valuation without hiring a single employee. This one-person company would instead be powered by AI and “employ” dozens of AI agents to do the work. Not only do I believe this is entirely possible, but I think when it does happen, the company will be one of the fastest-growing unicorns ever. As I invest in AI-powered startups and teach my students how to use AI in their businesses, I have identified 5 general AI use cases that align with critical phases of the startup journey: 1. Research-Driven Ideation: The genesis of any successful startup is a deep understanding of market needs, pain points, and the competitive landscape. My colleague Scott Brady of Stanford calls this process Research-Driven Ideation (RDI). There are now AI-based tools for competitive analysts, automating competitive monitoring for senior managers—effectively Google Alerts on steroids, tracking personnel changes, marketing launches, traffic, and other publicly available data. 2. Customer Persona Development and Market Research: Understanding your target customer is crucial. Gen AI helps founders create multiple hyper-specific customer personas by analyzing customer data and building hyper-realistic, "living" customer personas to test key hypotheses quickly. 3. Experimentation and Validation: Gen AI facilitates rapid experimentation to validate key hypotheses such as CVP, GTM, and PF by enabling deeper business data insights and rapid prototyping. I have a founder friend who lost his technical cofounder and has been using ChatGPT to build his MVP. By learning to be more effective at writing prompts to generate the desired code output, he has been able to continue building as a solo founder. He told me, “The result is that my burn rate is incredibly low, and velocity has shot through the roof.” 4. Marketing and Customer Engagement: Founders will see major productivity boosts in marketing, community building, and sales prospecting. Flybridge has a portfolio company that builds super smart AI agents that can be used for just about anything. One of their customers trained their agent to automatically generate customized sales collateral and follow-up materials based on customer needs that a sales representative inputs into the system after a prospect call—and then the AI agent sends that tailored material to the customer. 5. Continuous Learning and Iteration: The path to PMF is iterative. Gen AI supports continuous learning by analyzing customer feedback and product usage data to improve their product, GTM, and onboarding processes quickly. How are you using AI to build your startup?

In the corporate world, a well-crafted guesstimate isn’t just a number—it’s the difference between a nod of approval and a bigger bonus. The fudge factor term gained further traction in project management, where timelines and budgets often incorporated arbitrary buffers—e.g., multiplying expert estimates by 1.5x—to account for unforeseen delays. This practice, while ostensibly risk-averse, enabled planners to avoid scrutiny over optimistic assumptions by embedding unverified adjustments into baseline calculations.

In a recent blog post, Sam Altman shared profound insights from OpenAI's journey, reflecting on the challenges, lessons, and growth experienced while building groundbreaking technology. These reflections offer timeless lessons for navigating innovation and leadership: ✅️ Think long-term: True success comes from focusing on what will matter in the future. Building with purpose and vision creates lasting impact. ✅️ Embrace uncertainty: Progress is rarely linear—it's often two steps forward, one step back. This is especially true with emerging technologies. Growth comes from taking risks, adapting to challenges, and finding resilience in setbacks. ✅️ Empower people: Great leaders foster environments where teams can thrive and excel. ✅️ Diverse, experienced boards matter: A well-rounded board with complementary skills and networks is invaluable, especially during crises, offering the guidance and stability needed to navigate uncertainty. ✅️ Leverage the power of collaboration: In pivotal moments, having a strong support system of peers, mentors, and allies can make all the difference. Altman highlights how these networks helped prevent OpenAI from “falling apart” during challenging times. ✅️ Practice humility and recognize contributions: Acknowledging the efforts of others and understanding your own limits. This approach fosters trust, respect, and a balanced perspective under pressure. As Sam Altman notably states, " ..At the time, very few people cared, and if they did, it was mostly because they thought we had no chance of success." This serves as a powerful reminder that true vision often begins where others see impossibility. "In the last two years, we had to build an entire company almost from scratch around this new technology. There is no way to train people for this except by doing it..." The free tier of ChatGPT will get unlimited chat access to GPT-5 at the standard intelligence setting (!!), subject to abuse thresholds. It would be hard for Sam and the folks at OAI to ignore this truth. The gains in throwing more money at training is slowing down and it's very difficult to differentiate in this competitive market. While competition on the model side is getting more intense by the day, ChatGPT is killing it. 700M+ WAU (with an end year goal of 1B) and makes up for (by industry estimates) ~70% of OAI's revenues. And I admit, I'm a loyal ChatGPT subscriber and user. Why? It was one of the first products to launch memory. At this point, my ChatGPT instance knows everything about me, my family, my work, my hobbies, my lifestyle, my personality, everything. And I've gotten used to the way that it "thinks" (kudos to those ChatGPT prompt engineers). So much so that when I tried to actively switch to Gemini (signed up for the subscription for this very purpose), I gave up after just 1 day. It's almost like I got used to working with someone closely and trying to "switch" to working with someone else (who's maybe just as brilliant but just different, and knows nothing about me) is really really hard. It's terrifying. But also, I'm sure I'm not alone. Maybe folks who got used to Gemini find it hard to switch to ChatGPT? Anyone out there wanna share your experience? Anyway, all of its recent activities seem to indicate that Sam and team have decided to embrace this reality. That it's impossible to make it as a research lab, but it is possible to become a consumer tech giant with the huge footprint and consumer base they've got. I'm sure Google will put a good fight, but the pace at which OAI is launching products right now feels very early 2000s Google. Young upstart with infinite energy. Can the incumbent keep up?#AI#Tech

🔍 Unlocking the Difference: ChatGPT’s Internal Knowledge vs. Web Search ChatGPT used to be a “closed book” — its knowledge froze at its training cutoff date. But as of recent advances, it’s evolving into a hybrid system that blends memorized knowledge with live web access. Here’s what my article lays out: Static vs. live knowledge: Traditional models rely solely on their training data. Now, GPT variants can fetch and cite web info when needed. How web search got integrated: Starting with Bing API experiments, ChatGPT now includes a “Search the Web” feature, which selectively pulls in fresh data. Google’s role (and non-role): ChatGPT doesn’t inherently use Google’s backend or ranking. But third-party plugins can bridge that gap. Trade-offs, always: Real-time access boosts freshness and relevance, but risks like misinterpretation, bias, or erroneous citations remain — human oversight is vital. Timeline of evolution: From GPT-3.5’s static world to GPT-5 + SearchGPT’s live synthesis, my article traces major milestones. Ultimately, my piece argues that we’re entering a new era — one where AI and search converge. No longer are they rivals; they’re becoming one ecosystem. Read more: https://lnkd.in/gvvqsN3c#AI#ChatGPT#MachineLearning#WebSearch#SearchTechnology#AIintegration#DigitalInnovation#TechTrends
